Leaching is the process due to which important minerals of soil either wash away or they percolate into the lower layer. Laterite soil is formed due to leaching.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The black soil of the Deccan Plateau lacks which of the following minerals? [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]
solution:
(C) Phosphorus
Black Soil
Also known as Regur. Covers 15% area of the country.
It is clayey in nature and has self ploughing characteristics.
Suitable for cotton cultivation (requires 210 frost free days).
Formed due to eruption of lava.
Found over Deccan plateau region.
Rich in iron, lime, alumina and poor in humus, nitrogen, phosphate.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Punjab state is rich in which natural resource from the following? [SSC MTS 05/09/2023]
solution:
(D) Fertile soil
Punjab is rich in fertile soil and is known as the 'India's bread basket' due to its fertile land and agricultural productivity.
Do you know that 1st phase of Green Revolution started from Andhra Pradesh and Punjab?

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Laterite soil develops as a result of which of the following reasons? [SSC MTS 05/09/2023]
solution:
(B) Leaching
Laterite Soil
Laterite soil is also known as Brick soil.
Covers 4.3% area of India.
Formed by leaching process.
Soil is deficient in humus as well as important minerals such as silica.
Found in regions of high temperature and high rainfall.
Good for tea and coffee cultivation.
Found in Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh and Kerala.
Do you know that Karnataka is the 'coffee bowl of India'?

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The black soil area of the _______ is known as the Deccan Trap. [SSC MTS 06/09/2023]
solution:
(B) Peninsular Plateau
The black soil area of the peninsular plateau is known as the Deccan trap. It is good for cotton cultivation. It was formed due to eruption of ancient volcanoes on the Deccan plateau.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following crops is most suitable for black soil? [SSC MTS 11/09/2023]
solution:
(C) Cotton
Cotton is most suitable for black soil. It requires 210 frost free days and 6-8 months to mature. It is a Kharif crop (sown – July, harvested – September-October). It is a fibre crop and is known as 'Silver fibre'.
Silver fibre revolution - increased production and advancements in cotton industry.
Do you know that First Cotton Mill was established at Fort Gloster (Calcutta) in 1818?

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In which of the following states of India is the maximum extent of alluvial soil found? [SSC MTS 11/09/2023]
solution:
(B) Uttar Pradesh
Uttar Pradesh is the state with largest area under alluvial soil. Ganga river flows most distance through UP.
Major cities on the banks of Ganga (west to east) - Kanpur (largest), Prayagraj, Varanasi, Patna.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: What is the type of soil composed of silt and dust? [SSC CHSL 14/08/2023]
solution:
(C) Loess
Loess - Soil composed of silt and dust, formed due to wind blown deposits, found in areas with dry climate and sparse vegetation, highly fertile.
Loam - It is a soil formed due to mixture of clay, sand and silt.
Marl - Soft, light coloured, mud like sediment seen in shallow waters.
Podsol - Soil which are characterised by distinct soil horizons.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following soil requires minimum tillage because of its self-cultivating feature? [SSC MTS 11/05/2023]
solution:
(D) Black soil
Black soil is known for its self-cultivating feature as it develops cracks when it is dry and becomes sticky when it is wet and hence requires minimum tillage.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The colour of the alluvial soils varies from _______ [SSC MTS 13/06/2023]
solution:
(D) Light grey to ash grey
The colour of the alluvial soil varies from light grey to ash grey.
The colour of the red soil changes to yellow on hydration.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is an example of a micronutrient supplied by soil? [SSC MTS 13/06/2023]
solution:
(B) Iron
Iron is an example of micronutrient supplied by soil.
Macronutrient – Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium, Calcium, Magnesium, Sulfur.
Rest other are micronutrients.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following statements is NOT correct regarding fertilizers? [SSC MTS 14/06/2023]

options.b: They are very rich in plant nutrients
options.c: They provide humus to the soil

solution:
(C) They provide humus to the soil
Fertilizers are inorganic salts which are prepared in factories. They are very rich in plant nutrients. Fertilizers don't provide humus (organic material obtained by decomposition of plants and animals) to the soil.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Arid soils are characteristically developed in _______, which exhibit characteristic arid topography. [SSC CHSL 09/03/2023]
solution:
(C) Western Rajasthan
See solution of question 2 (level 1).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following pairs of 'soil - characteristics' is correct? [SSC CHSL 13/03/2023]
I. Sandy - proportion of fine particles is relatively higher
II. Clayey - greater proportion of big particles
solution:
(B) Neither I nor II
Sandy soil has higher proportion of big particles while clayey soil has higher proportion of fine particles.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following factors of soil formation determines the accumulation of the soil? [SSC CHSL 14/03/2023]
solution:
(C) Relief
Factors responsible for the formation of soil
Relief - determines accumulation of soil.
Parent rock/Bed rock - determines the soil's colour, texture, chemical properties, mineral content and permeability.
Climate - temperature and rainfall affect the rate of weathering and humus.
Time - determines the thickness of soil.
Vegetation
Do you know that study of soil is called Pedology while Petrology is the study of rocks?

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In which of the soil conservation methods in the coastal and dry regions, rows of trees are planted to check the wind movement to protect soil cover? [SSC CHSL 17/03/2023]
solution:
(B) Shelter belts
Soil Conservation Methods
Shelter Belts - rows of trees are planted to check the wind movement to protect the soil cover.
Contour Bunding - practiced in sloppy and hilly areas where lines of stone are placed along the natural rise.
Mulching - practice of covering the top soil with plant material so that soil retains moisture.
Terrace Farming - method of growing crops on sides of hills or mountains by planting on graduated terraces built into the slope.
Strip Farming - crops are grown in alternate pattern.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following statements is NOT correct regarding laterite soil? [SSC CHSL 20/03/2023]

options.a: Red laterite soils in Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh and Kerala are more suitable for crops like cashew nuts.
options.b: They are generally rich in humus.
options.c: This soil is very useful for growing tea and coffee.
options.d: The laterite soil develops in areas with high temperature and heavy rainfall.

solution:
(B) They are generally rich in humus
See solution of question 8 (level 1).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: What do you call the weight a soil can withstand before severe damage occurs to the structure of the soil? [SSC CGL 18/04/2022]
solution:
(B) Bearing capacity
Bearing capacity is the weight a soil can withstand before severe damage occurs to the structure of the soil.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: _______ develops on crystalline igneous rocks in areas of low rainfall in the eastern and southern part of the Deccan Plateau. [SSC CGL 20/04/2022]
solution:
(A) Red soil
Red Soil
Covers 18% area of India.
Formed by the weathering of metamorphic rocks.
Red due to presence of Iron oxide.
Changes colour to yellow on hydration.
Found in areas with low rainfall.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which part of the soil contains humus? [SSC MTS 06/10/2021]
solution:
(A) A-horizon
Soil Horizons
O - Organic layer (humus), absent in Indian soils
A - Topsoil (minerals with humus)
B - Subsoil (deposited minerals & metal salts)
C - Parent rock/Bed rock, soil texture depends on it

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: _______ is a deep red soil found in tropical regions and often developed on mafic igneous bedrock. [SSC CHSL 16/04/2021]
solution:
(C) Laterite
Laterite is a deep red soil found in tropical regions and often developed on mafic igneous bedrock (bedrock rich in magnesium and iron).
Pedocal - soil formed in drier regions, less fertile, rich in calcium carbonate.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: As per 'Soils of India, National Bureau of Soil Survey and Land Use Planning report: Publication Number 94', which type of soil (order) is most abundantly available? [SSC CHSL 16/04/2021]
solution:
(C) Inceptisols
Inceptisols are the recently formed soils that lack well developed horizons and are commonly found on unconsolidated river alluvial/beach sediments of sand. They are most abundantly available in India.
Alfisols - soils with aluminium and iron.
These are USDA taxonomy. Other terms of USDA taxonomy - Vertisols, Oxisol, Gelisol, Histosol.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following statements is NOT correct about alluvial soil? [SSC CHSL 12/08/2021]

options.a: Deltas of Mahanadi, the Godavari and the Krishna are made from alluvial soil.
options.b: Old alluvial is called 'bangar' and new alluvial is called 'khadar'.
options.c: In the Himalayan region we find alluvial soil.
options.d: Northern plains are made of alluvial soil.

solution:
(C) In the Himalayan region we find alluvial soil
See solution of question 4 (level 1).
Himalayan region has montane soil (rich in humus), not alluvial soil.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is INCORRECTLY paired? [SSC CHSL 12/10/2020]
solution:
(D) Pedology - Phyto geography
Anthropology - Cultural geography :- both are related to animals and their culture
Demography - Population geography :- both are related to population of humans
Hydroloby - Oceanography :- both are related to water
Pedology is the study of soils while Phyto geography is the study of plants.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: What was the main purpose of establishing the Agricultural Prices Commission in 1965? [SSC CPO 28/06/2024]

options.a: To determine the price of fertiliser and electricity paid by farmers.
options.b: To determine the price of seeds used in agriculture.
options.c: To determine the prices for various farm products purchased by the government.
options.d: To determine the prices for various farm products for sale in the open market.

solution:
(C) To determine the prices for various farm products purchased by the government.
Agricultural Prices Commission (APC) was established on 1 January 1965.
Purpose - to recommend MSPs for major agricultural products.
Renamed – in 1985, APC became Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP). Function - advised the government on pricing policies for agricultural commodities.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is an INCORRECTLY stated social impact of the Green Revolution? [SSC CPO 28/06/2024]

options.a: Only farmers involved in subsistence agriculture were benefitted.
options.b: Primarily, the medium and large farmers were benefited.
options.c: Worsened the condition of landless and marginal farmers.
options.d: There was displacement of service caste group.

solution:
(A) Only farmers involved in subsistence agriculture were benefitted.
Green revolution refers to a sudden increase in food grain (wheat and rice) production, primarily due to use of HYV seeds and irrigation facility. It benefitted primarily the large farmers because of high input cost on HYV seeds, fertilisers etc.
About Green revolution :
1st in Mexico and Latin America (1940s-60s).
Term given by Wiliam Gaud. Father of green revolution - Norman Borlaug (USA, Nobel peace prize 1970), in India - MS Swaminathan.
It was introduced in India in 2 phases - 1st phase (1960s-1970s) - in affluent states of Punjab, Haryana, Andhra Pradesh. 2nd phase (1970s-80s) - focus on sustainable farming.
Do you know that the headquarters of 2nd green revolution was ICAR, Patna?

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The golden revolution in India related to the agriculture sector is referred to as: [SSC PHASE XII 24/06/2024]
solution:
(C) Horticulture and honey
Agriculture Revolutions in India
Golden revolution - fruits/honey
Black revolution - petroleum
Green revolution - food grains
Brown revolution - leather
Blue revolution - fishery
Grey revolution - fertilizer
Yellow revolution - oil seeds
Pink revolution - onion
White revolution (operation flood) - milk
Red revolution - meat/tomato
Silver revolution - egg/poultry
Evergreen revolution - overall development
Father of white revolution - Verghese Kurien.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which crops are benefitted from the temperate cyclones arising from the Mediterranean Sea that cause rainfall in Punjab? [SSC PHASE XII 21/06/2024]
solution:
(B) Rabi crops
Rabi crops are benefitted from the rainfall caused by the temperate cyclones arising from the Mediterranean sea.
Rabi Crops
Sowing - October, Harvesting - April
Eg. Wheat, Barley, Gram, Mustard, Linseed, Pea, Rapeseed, Castor.
Zaid Crops
Short cropping season (May-June)
Eg. Watermelon, Muskmelon, Cucumber, Fodder crops.
Do you know that India's most consumed staple crops are wheat and rice?

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In which Union Territory is the 'slash and burn' agriculture known as 'Dipa'? [SSC JE 05/06/2024]

options.c: Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu

solution:
(A) Andaman & Nicobar Islands
In slash and burn agriculture, the farmers clear a patch of land and produce cereals and other food crops to sustain their family. When the soil fertility decreases, the farmers shift and clear a fresh patch of land for cultivation.
Different names of Shifting cultivation
Northeast - Jhum
Chhattisgarh and A&N islands - Dipa
Western Ghats - Kumari
Rajasthan - Waltre
Odisha - Pama Dabi/Bringa
Jharkhand - Kuruwa
Andhra Pradesh - Penda/Podu
Madhya Pradesh - Bewar/Dahiya
Across world
Indonesia - Ladang, Mexico - Milpa, Sri Lanka - Chena, Vietnam - Ray, Brazil - Roca, Venezuela - Konuko, Zaire river valley - Masole, Thailand - Tamarai.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following types of crops are sown at the beginning of the monsoon season? [SSC CHSL 08/07/2024]
solution:
(C) Kharif crops
Kharif Crops
Sowing – July, Harvesting - September October, also known as 'Monsoon crops'.
Eg. Rice, Sugarcane, Jute, Cotton, Tobacco, Maize, Soyabean, Groundnut.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following statements correctly defines the green revolution? [SSC CGL 09/09/2024]

options.a: It is a new strategy in agriculture to produce food grains, especially wheat and rice.
options.b: It is a new strategy to increase the share of the forest.
options.c: It is a new strategy to use green colour for all purposes.
options.d: It is a new strategy to use only herbal products.

solution:
(A) It is a new strategy in agriculture to produce food grains, especially wheat and rice.
Green revolution refers to a sudden increase in food grain (wheat and rice) production, primarily due to use of HYV seeds and irrigation facility. It benefitted primarily the large farmers because of high input cost on HYV seeds, fertilisers etc.
Drawbacks of Green Revolution
Drop in groundwater level.
Reduction in soil fertility.
Increase in soil alkalinity.
Disparity between farmers increased as commercial farmers benefitted more.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is NOT true with regard to the Green Revolution? [SSC CPO 29/06/2024]

options.a: In its second phase, there was a shift from dry to wet (irrigated) cultivation in dry and semi-arid regions of India.
options.b: Cultivation became more intensive in nature.
options.c: Cultivation became more extensive in nature.
options.d: There was change in the cropping pattern and types of crops grown in its second phase.

solution:
(C) Cultivation became more extensive in nature
Green revolution focused on intensification of agriculture (increasing yield per unit area) rather than extending farming (increasing area under cultivation).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In 2018, India was the _______ producer of groundnut in the world. [SSC CHSL 14/03/2023]
solution:
(D) Second largest
Groundnut is a type of oilseed. Other eg of oilseeds - coconut, mustard, sesame, cotton seed, soyabean.
In 2018, India became the 2nd largest producer of groundnut in the world after China, 3rd in the list was Nigeria followed by USA.
Gujarat is the largest producer of groundnut in India.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following was true with regard to the Green Revolution? [SSC CPO 27/06/2024]

options.a: It was targeted mainly at the pulses-growing areas.
options.b: Its method of cultivation required less quantity of water.
options.c: It involved use of high-yielding variety of seeds.
options.d: It involved use of organic fertilisers and pesticides.

solution:
(C) It involved use of high-yielding variety of seeds
See solution of question 35 (level 1).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Norman Ernest Borlaug was awarded the _______ in 1970 for his contributions to the green revolution. [SSC CHSL 03/07/2024]
solution:
(A) Nobel Peace Prize
See solution of question 30 (level 1).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Who among the following is NOT associated with the Green Revolution? [SSC CHSL 11/07/2024]
solution:
(A) P Chidambaram
P Chidambaram isn't associated with Green Revolution. Norman Borlaug (father of green revolution, conferred with Nobel Peace Prize in 1970), Chidambaram Subramaniam (conferred with Bharat Ratna in 1998) and MS Swaminathan (father of green revolution in India) are associated with green revolution.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The Green Revolution led to an increase in the agricultural output primarily due to the use of HYV seeds. Here, HYV refers to: [SSC CHSL 03/07/2024]
solution:
(D) High Yielding Variety
HYV (High Yielding Variety) seeds were used in Green revolution to increase the agricultural output. Earlier used seeds (PL480) were imported from USA.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The Green Revolution was due to the introduction of: [SSC CHSL 03/07/2024]
solution:
(C) High yielding varieties of seeds
The Green Revolution was due to the introduction of HYV (high yielding variety) seeds.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In July 1968, Indira Gandhi, the then Prime Minister of India, officially recorded impressive strides of the Green Revolution in agriculture by releasing a special stamp entitled _______. [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(A) Wheat Revolution
In July 1968, Indira Gandhi released a special stamp entitled 'Wheat Revolution' to acknowledge the success of green revolution.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following reasons can be attributed to the excessive pressure on agricultural land in India?
I. Right of Inheritance
II. Size of unprofitable land holding
III. Division of land
IV. Multiple cropping
solution:
(C) I, III and IV
Factors responsible for excessive pressure on agricultural land in India - Right of inheritance, division of land, multiple cropping, huge population etc.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following countries is related with high yielding variety seeds that are used as part of Green Revolution policies in India? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(B) Mexico
During green revolution, PL-480 variety of wheat was imported from Mexico and IR8 variety (miracle rice) of rice was imported from Philippines.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following has NOT been a positive impact of the Green Revolution in India? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(D) Non-food grains excluded
Green revolution focused mainly on food grains (especially wheat and rice), neglecting non food grains. It is a negative aspect of green revolution.
Positive impacts of the Green revolution
Increased food grain production.
Reduced import of food grains.
Benefit to farmers.
Industrial growth.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following varieties of rice is known as the miracle rice for India and was first harvested in 1967 with exceptional yield? [SSC STENOGRAPHER 13/10/2023]
solution:
(A) IR8
IR8 rice is known as Miracle rice. It was imported from Philippines.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: For Kharif crops, the temperature required for farming is above 25 degrees Celsius with high humidity and annual rainfall above _______ cm. [SSC CPO 03/10/2023]
solution:
(C) 100
Rice
Called paddy before processing.
India is the 2nd largest producer after China.
Sowed in swampy areas - waterlogged conditions (presence of methane).
Requires high temperature (>25°C) and high rainfall (>100cm).
Aus, Aman and Boro are the three different varieties of rice sown during different seasons.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Identify the group of major Jute producing states of India. [SSC CPO 04/10/2023]

options.c: Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Madhya Pradesh

solution:
(B) West Bengal, Bihar, Assam, Odisha
Jute :
Also called 'Golden Fibre'. Nor-westers are good for its growth.
Top producer - India (West Bengal), top exporter - Bangladesh.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: _______ land is suitable for regular cropping. [SSC CPO 05/10/2023]
solution:
(A) Arable
Arable land is the land which is suitable for growing crops while barren land is the one which is infertile and hence not suitable for cropping.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Shifting agriculture is known as _______ in the Western Ghats. [SSC PO 05/10/2023]
solution:
(A) Kumari
See solution of question 33 (level 1).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which group of crops belong to fibre crops and are produced on a large scale in India? [SSC MTS 06/09/2023]
solution:
(B) Jute and Hemp
Fibre crops include - cotton, jute, hemp, silk (rearing of silk - Sericulture).
Cotton :
Also called as 'Silver Fibre'. Requires black soil, 210 frost free days and 6-8 months to mature.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In India, the 'Aus', 'Aman' and 'Boro' terms are associated with which crop from the following? [SSC MTS 11/09/2023]
solution:
(D) Paddy
Aus, Aman and Boro are the three different varieties of rice grown during different seasons.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following crops is the only genetically modified (GM) crop approved for commercial cultivation in India? [SSC CHSL 02/08/2023]
solution:
(C) Bt Cotton
Genetically Modified (GM) crops are the crops whose DNA is modified using genetic engineering techniques.
Eg. Bt Brinjal, Bt Cotton etc.
Bt Cotton is the only GM crop which has been approved for commercial cultivation in India.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of these is true about the second phase of the green revolution? [SSC CHSL 03/08/2023]

options.a: It covered the period of the 1980s.
options.b: It was introduced in areas with irrigation facilities.
options.c: It covered the period of the 1960s and 1970s.
options.d: It was introduced in Punjab and Uttar Pradesh regions.

solution:
(A) It covered the period of the 1980s
Second phase of green revolution was introduced in 1970s-80s. It focused more on sustainable farming, extensive use of HYV seed. Most of the crops were benefitted by this.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following crops can be grown during the Kharif season in India? [SSC CHSL 03/08/2023]
solution:
(D) Cotton
Cotton is a kharif crop.
Cotton
Also called as 'Silver Fibre'. Requires black soil, 210 frost free days and 6-8 months to mature.
Wheat - requires 50-70 cm of rainfall

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Cultivating large farms with moderately low inputs of labour and capital is referred to as _______. [SSC CHSL 04/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Extensive cultivation
Extensive cultivation - practiced on large farms with low inputs of labour and capital
Intensive cultivation - method used to maximise the crop production per unit area by using high inputs of labor, capital and technology.
Hybrid cultivation - it combines different agricultural techniques like vertical farming and traditional methods to maximise the yield while also addressing the environmental challenges.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Match the columns. [SSC CHSL 08/08/2023]
Names of Shifting Agriculture - Related State
I. Jhuming - a. Madhya Pradesh
II. Dahiya - b. Andhra Pradesh
III. Penda - c. North eastern region
IV. Kumari - d. Western Ghats
solution:
(D) I-c, II-a, III-b, IV-d
Jhuming - North Eastern region
Dahiya - Madhya Pradesh
Penda - Andhra Pradesh
Kumari - Western Ghats

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is the most suitable application of rainwater harvesting? [SSC CHSL 09/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Irrigation
Rainwater harvesting is the process of collecting, storing and using rainwater. Irrigation is the most suitable application of rainwater harvesting.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: What is meant by subsistence agriculture? [SSC CHSL 09/08/2023]

options.a: Agricultural output is sufficient for the country.
options.d: Agriculturists produce primarily for the market.

solution:
(B) Agriculturists produce primarily for themselves
Subsistence agriculture means to produce crops for one's own use, without any surplus. It is done mainly by manual labor. Subsistence farming is of 2 types - Primitive subsistence farming and Intensive subsistence farming.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the negative effects of the green revolution? [SSC CHSL 09/08/2023]

options.a: Primarily medium and large farmers were able to benefit from the new technology of the green revolution.
options.b: The green revolution benefited farmers who had access to land, capital, technology, and know-how.
options.c: The green revolution led to the displacement of tenant cultivators in many cases.
options.d: The green revolution caused increased migration from urban to rural areas.

solution:
(D) The green revolution caused increased migration from urban to rural areas.
The green revolution caused migration of people from rural to urban areas as the increased mechanisation led to reduced need for labor, forcing them to migrate towards urban areas.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following states of India still uses the ancient bamboo drip irrigation method? [SSC CHSL 11/08/2023]
solution:
(B) Meghalaya
The ancient bamboo drip irrigation method is practiced in Meghalaya. The system taps water sources using bamboo channels and transports the water to the plants, delivering water in a controlled manner.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following classification bases of industry applies to the products - cotton, jute, wool, silk textile, rubber, tea, sugar, coffee, edible oil? [SSC CHSL 11/08/2023]
solution:
(C) Agro-based Industries
Agro-based Industries - use agricultural products as their raw materials.
Mineral-based Industries - use mineral ores as their raw materials.
Key industries - industries essential to the functioning of other industries. Eg. machine making industry.
Consumer industries - industries which produce goods and services intended for direct use by the consumers.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: 54.6% of the total workforce is engaged in _______ and allied sector activities (Census of India 2011). [SSC CHSL 14/08/2023]
solution:
(C) Agriculture
Primary sector (agriculture and allied activities) engages 54.6% population and has a share of 14.39% in GDP of the country.
Secondary sector - engages 24.3% population, contributes 31.46% to GDP
Tertiary sector - engages 21.1% population, contributes 54.15% to GDP.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of these is NOT true about the green revolution? [SSC CHSL 14/08/2023]

options.a: It was funded by international agencies.
options.b: It was a private-sector programme for agricultural modernization.
options.c: It was targeted mainly in the wheat and rice-growing areas.
options.d: It was introduced only in areas that had assured irrigation.

solution:
(B) It was a private-sector programme for agricultural modernization.
Green revolution was a government led initiative aimed at increasing the production of food grains (especially wheat and rice). It was introduced in areas which had assured irrigation as sufficient irrigation was necessary for HYV seeds. It was funded by international agencies.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is a plantation crop found abundantly in Assam? [SSC CHSL 17/08/2023]
solution:
(A) Tea
Plantation crops - tea, coffee, rubber, sugarcane, banana etc.
Tea :
Grown in acidic soil. Requires well drained soil and shadow.
Top producers - China > India > Kenya
In India, Assam is the topmost producer.
Duars (alluvial floodplains in the NE India lying in the foothills of eastern Himalayas) are major contributors to the Indian tea industry.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of these is NOT one of the impacts of the green revolution? [SSC CHSL 17/08/2023]

options.a: A loosening of traditional bonds or hereditary relationships between farmers or landowners and agricultural workers.
options.b: A shift from payment in cash to payment in kind (grain).
options.c: The rise of a class of 'free' wage labourers.
options.d: There was an increase in the use of agricultural labour as cultivation became more intensive.

solution:
(B) A shift from payment in cash to payment in kind (grain)
During green revolution, payment started to be done in cash in place of kind (grain).
Disguised unemployment is more visible in primary sector.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following climatic conditions are required for rice cultivation? [SSC MTS 04/05/2023]
solution:
(A) High temperature and high humidity
Rice needs high temperature (>25°C), high humidity and high rainfall (> 100 cm).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: _______ was introduced on the Baba Budan Hills? [SSC CGL 18/07/2023]
solution:
(C) Coffee
Coffee :
Introduced on Baba Budan hills (in Chikkamagaluru district of Karnataka) in India.
Topmost producer - Brazil (coffee bowl of the world). In India - Karnataka (3 varieties - Arabica, Robusta, Liberica).
Requires laterite soil. Blossom showers help in growth of coffee.
India produces 3.2% of the world's coffee (2008).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following have been characteristics of the Green Revolution? [SSC CGL 19/07/2023]
I. Rise in crop productivity
II. Shift from commercial farming to subsistence farming
III. Increase in acreage
solution:
(B) Only I and III
Green revolution led to the increase in crop productivity, rise in acreage (area under cultivation) and shift from subsistence farming to commercial farming.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following is a negative effect of the Green Revolution in India? [SSC CGL 20/07/2023]
I. Depletion of groundwater table
II. Deterioration in the quality of soil
III. Increased input cost
solution:
(C) I, II and III
Negative effects of green revolution
Depletion of groundwater table.
Deterioration in the quality of soil.
Increased input cost as the new technology, HYV seeds were costly.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Dr. D.S. Athwal was known as the 'Father of ________ Revolution' in India. [SSC CGL 21/07/2023]
solution:
(D) Wheat
Dr. D.S. Athwal was known as the 'Father of Wheat Revolution' in India.
Father of Blue revolution - Harilal Chaudhuri and Arun Krishnan
Father of White revolution - Verghese Kurien
Father of Yellow revolution - Sam Pitroda

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the characteristics of the new agricultural strategy? [SSC CGL 26/07/2023]
solution:
(D) Use of organic manures
Characteristics of new agricultural strategy - Use of chemical fertilisers, Use of hybrid seeds, Extensive irrigation.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The first phase of the Green Revolution was restricted largely to two crops and the regions where they are grown. Which of the following pairs best represents these two crops? [SSC CGL 26/07/2023]
solution:
(B) Wheat, rice
The first phase of green revolution was limited to wheat and rice.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In which type of farming, the land is used for growing food and fodder crops and rearing livestock? [SSC MTS 02/05/2023]
solution:
(C) Mixed farming
Mixed Farming - farming which involves growing of crops and raising of livestocks.
Intercropping - growing of multiple crops on the same land in a specific sequence.
Mixed cropping - growing of multiple crops on the same land without any specific sequence.
Commercial agriculture - growing crops primarily for sale.
Plantation agriculture - type of commercial agriculture which specializes in growing single crop.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is NOT an example of Millets or coarse grains? [SSC MTS 02/05/2023]
solution:
(C) Wheat
Millets (Coarse grains)
also known as superfoods/sri anna as they are good source of minerals, dietary fibre, antioxidants and protein.
Topmost producer - Rajasthan.
Eg. Bajra, Jowar (Sorghum), Ragi (finger millets) (TRICK - BAJRA)
Do you know that Food and Agricultural Organisation and United Nations declared 2023 as the 'International Year of Millets'?

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: 'Agricultural Census' is conducted in India at an interval of ______ years. [SSC MTS 04/05/2023]
solution:
(B) Five years
Agricultural census is done every 5 years. It is conducted by Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare. The latest one (11th) was done in 2021-22.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following is defined as 'sericulture'? [SSC MTS 08/05/2023]
solution:
(D) Commercial rearing of silk worms
Sericulture - rearing of silk worms
Apiculture - bee keeping
Horticulture - growing of fruits and vegetables
Silviculture - growing of trees
Pisciculture - rearing of fishes
Viticulture - growing of grapes
Floriculture - growing of flowers
Pomiculture - growing of fruits
Arboriculture - cultivation of trees and shrubs

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The technique of watering plants by making use of narrow tubes which deliver water directly at the base of the plant is called _______. [SSC MTS 10/05/2023]
solution:
(C) Drip irrigation
Drip irrigation - water is given drop by drop directly at the base of the plant, minimising water usage.
Indira Gandhi canal - longest canal in India, starts from Harike barrage and provides irrigation facilities in Thar desert.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which type of farming is practiced to meet the needs of the farmer's family? [SSC MTS 19/05/2023]
solution:
(B) Subsistence farming
Subsistence agriculture means to produce crops for one's own use, without any surplus. It is done mainly by manual labor with little or no technological input.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In which of the following methods of irrigation, the water falls drop by drop directly near the roots? [SSC MTS 19/05/2023]
solution:
(D) Drip system
Drip irrigation - water is given drop by drop directly at the base of the plant, minimising water usage.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: There are regions where farmers specialize in vegetables only. This type of farming is known as _______. [SSC MTS 19/05/2023]
solution:
(D) Truck farming
Truck farming refers to growing vegetables on a large scale, for transportation to distant places by using trucks.
Pastoral farming/Livestock farming - focuses on raising domesticated animals for meat, milk, wool, or other products, rather than growing crops.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which among the following terms means growing of fruits? [SSC MTS 14/06/2023]
solution:
(A) Pomiculture
See solution of question 78 (level 1).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Jhum cultivation or shifting cultivation is also known by the name of _______. [SSC MTS 15/06/2023]
solution:
(D) Slash and burn agriculture
Jhum / Shifting cultivation is also known as Slash and burn agriculture.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: India is the _______ producer of pulses and the _______ consumer of pulses. [SSC CHSL 10/03/2023]
solution:
(A) Largest, largest
Leguminous crops
India is the largest producer and largest consumer.
Nitrogen fixing bacteria (Rhizobacter, Nostoc, Anabena) resides in the roots of these plants except Rajma.
Eg. Pulses, Rajma (no bacteria), Soyabean (top producer - MP).
In millet production - India (Rajasthan) > China > Nigeria.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Alluvial soils vary in nature from sandy loam to clay. They are generally: [CDS I 2021]

options.a: Poor in potash and rich in phosphorus.
options.b: Poor in both potash and phosphorus.
options.c: Rich in both potash and phosphorus.
options.d: Rich in potash and poor in phosphorus.

solution:
(D) Rich in potash and poor in phosphorus
Alluvial soils are rich in potash and poor in phosphorus (agar phosphorus mein rich hogi to sab log matchsticks bna lege).
Types :- Old alluvial (Bhangar) - calcareous concentration, poor in humus and nitrogen (less fertile) New alluvial (Khadar) - more fertile.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Identify the crop on the basis of the following characteristics:
1. It is a kharif crop.
2. Aus, Aman and Boro are its three different growing periods in an agricultural year.
3. About one-fourth of the total cropped area of India is under its cultivation.
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [CDS I 2021]
solution:
(B) Rice
Rice is a kharif crop having three different growing periods - Aus, Aman and Boro. About one-fourth of the total cropped area of India is under its cultivation.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Milpa and Ladang are different names for: [CDS I 2020]
solution:
(A) Shifting cultivation
Different names of shifting cultivation across the world :
Indonesia - Ladang, Mexico - Milpa, Sri Lanka - Chena, Vietnam - Ray, Brazil - Roca, Venezuela - Konuko, Zaire river valley - Masole, Thailand - Tamarai.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Identify the type of soil on the basis of the given characteristics:
1. They are rich in lime, iron, magnesia and alumina.
2. They are generally clayey, deep and impermeable.
3. They are mainly found in Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh and Gujarat.
Select the correct answer from the given alternatives: [CDS I 2021]
solution:
(D) Black soil
Black soil (Regur) is rich in lime, iron, magnesia, alumina and poor in humus, nitrogen, phosphate. It is clayey, impermeable and shows self ploughing characteristics. It is found in regions of Maharashtra, Gujarat and MP. It is good for cotton cultivation.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Arabica, Robusta and Liberica are varieties of: [CDS I 2021]
solution:
(A) Coffee
Coffee :
Introduced on Baba Budan hills in India.
Topmost producer - Brazil (coffee bowl of the world). In India - Karnataka (3 varieties - Arabica, Robusta, Liberica).
Arabica received GI tag in 2019.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following States is not prominent for plantation agriculture? [CDS I 2021]
solution:
(A) Rajasthan
Plantation agriculture means growing of the same crop year round on a large scale. Major plantation crops include coffee, tea, jute, cotton, cashew, sugarcane etc.
Rajasthan is not a prominent state for plantation agriculture due to its arid and semi-arid climate.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: S had witnessed contour bunding and contour ploughing while visiting one of the States in India. Identify the State from the options given below. [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(C) Himachal Pradesh
Contour bunding is the practice of placing lines of stones along the slope of the hill to prevent soil from eroding.
Contour ploughing is the practice of growing crops along the slope to prevent soil erosion.
These are soil conservation processes practised in hilly areas.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Inceptisols, Entisols, Vertisols and Mollisols are orders of which one of the following? [CDS II 2021]
solution:
(B) Soil
Inceptisols, Entisols, Vertisols and Mollisols are types of soil according to US taxonomy.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the code given below the lists: [CDS I 2022]
List-I (Soil Type) - List-II (Salient Characteristic)
a. Entisols - 1. Soils having weakly or quickly developed horizons
b. Inceptisols - 2. Weakly developed soils with no horizons
c. Histosols - 3. These soils are very old and highly weathered
d. Oxisols - 4. These soils have very high quantity of organic matter in the upper layers
solution:
(B) a-2, b-1, c-4, d-3
Entisols - Weakly developed soils with no horizons
Inceptisols - Soils having weakly or quickly developed horizons
Histosols - These soils have very high quantity of organic matter in the upper layers
Oxisols - These soils are very old and highly weathered

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following characteristics of a soil type:
1. These soils consist of various proportions of sand, silt and clay.
2. These soils contain adequate proportions of potash, phosphoric acid and lime.
3. These soils can be further classified on the basis of their age.
Identify the type of soil on the basis of the given characteristics. [CDS I 2023]
solution:
(A) Alluvial
Alluvial soils consist of various proportions of sand, silt and clay. These soils are rich in potash, phosphoric acid and lime but poor in phosphorus and organic matter. These soils can be further classified on the basis of their age.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following crops is not cultivated under dryland farming? [CDS II 2023]
solution:
(C) Sugarcane
Sugarcane is not cultivated under dryland farming. It requires 75-100 cm of rainfall and 21°C-27°C temperature.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Identify the type of soil on the basis of given characteristics: [CDS II 2023]
1. They contain a large proportion of sodium, potassium and magnesium, and thus, they are infertile.
2. Their structure ranges from sandy to loamy.
3. They are more widespread in western Gujarat, deltas of the eastern coast and in Sundarban areas of West Bengal.
solution:
(D) Saline soil
Saline soil contains large proportion of sodium, potassium and magnesium and thus is infertile. Their structure ranges from sandy to loamy. They are spread across western Gujarat, deltas of eastern coast and in Sundarban area of West Bengal.
Marshy / Peaty soil - seen in coastal areas, has organic matter, seen in regions of high humidity and high rainfall.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following statements about plantation agriculture is not correct? [CDS II 2023]

options.a: Plantation farm estates are scientifically managed.
options.b: Plantation farming requires heavy capital outlay.
options.c: Plantation farm lands have mainly foreign ownership and local labour.
options.d: Plantation farmers rear the animals for sale.

solution:
(D) Plantation farmers rear the animals for sale
Plantation agriculture is growing of a single crop on a large scale all round the year. Plantation farm estates are scientifically managed and required heavy capital. Plantation farm lands have mainly foreign ownership and local labour.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the code given below the lists: [CDS II 2023]
List-I (Name of Shifting Cultivation) - List-II (Country)
a. Roca - 1. Sri Lanka
b. Chena - 2. Thailand
c. Masole - 3. Brazil
d. Tamarai - 4. Zaire
solution:
(D) a-3, b-1, c-4, d-2
Different names of shifting cultivation across the world :- Indonesia - Ladang, Mexico - Milpa, Sri Lanka - Chena, Vietnam - Ray, Brazil - Roca, Venezuela - Konuko, Zaire river valley - Masole, Thailand - Tamarai.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following pairs of revolution in agriculture and its production are correctly matched?
1. Pink revolution : Shrimp production
2. Yellow revolution : Oilseeds production
3. Black revolution : Biodiesel production
4. Golden revolution : Egg production
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [CDS II 2023]
solution:
(A) 1, 2 and 3
Pink revolution - shrimp, onion, meat, poultry
Yellow revolution - oilseeds
Black revolution - biodiesel, petroleum
Golden revolution - honey, horticulture
Silver revolution - egg

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: What is culturable wasteland? [CDS I 2024]

options.a: Land that has been left fallow in last four years.
options.b: Land that has been left fallow between one and two years.
options.c: Land that has been left fallow for less than one year.
options.d: Land that has been left fallow for more than five years.

solution:
(D) Land that has been left fallow for more than five years.
Different types of land according to land use :
Forests, Barren and wastelands, Land put to non-agricultural uses, Area under permanent pastures and grazing lands, Area under miscellaneous tree crops and groves, Culturable wasteland (fallow for more than 5 years), Current fallow (fallow for one year or less), Fallow other than current fallow (fallow for 1-5 years), Net area sown.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In which one of the following soils, the salt content is so high that common salt is obtained by evaporating the saline water in some areas? [CAPF 2022]
solution:
(D) Arid soil
Arid soils are saline in nature and in some areas, salt content is so high that salt is obtained by evaporating the saline water.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: 'Operation Flood' is also popularly known as: [CAPF 2022]
solution:
(B) White Revolution
White Revolution/Operation Flood - launched in 1970, father : Verghese Kurien, increase in milk production

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In which one of the following States, more than 50% area is under Jhum cultivation? [CAPF 2022]
solution:
(D) Mizoram
Mizoram has more than 50% of area under Jhum cultivation.
Recall different names of shifting cultivation across India and across the world.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following statements about Laterite soil found in India:
1. Laterite soil is found in high altitudes of Himalaya.
2. It is a very fertile soil.
3. It is a red colour soil due to the presence of iron oxide.
4. It is poor in Nitrogen, Potash and organic matter.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct? [CAPF 2023]
solution:
(C) 3 and 4 only
Laterite soil is also called as Brick soil and is found in areas of Western and Eastern ghats. It is generally infertile due to leaching of minerals. It is poor in nitrogen, potash, organic matter, lime, phosphorus etc. but rich in iron and aluminium oxides. It is red in colour due to presence of iron oxides. It is good for cashew and coffee cultivation.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Using the code given below identify the type of farming on the basis of given characteristics:
1. The produce is consumed entirely or mainly by the family who work the land or tend the livestock.
2. If a small surplus is produced, it may be sold or bartered.
3. It is generally small scale and labour intensive with little or no technological input. [CAPF 2023]
solution:
(C) Subsistence farming
Subsistence agriculture means to produce crops for one's own use, without any surplus. It is done mainly by manual labor with little or no technological input.
Subsistence farming is of 2 types - Primitive subsistence farming (2 types - shifting cultivation and nomadic herding) and Intensive subsistence farming.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following statements about millets:
1. Millets are often referred to as climate-resilient crops because they can grow on arid lands with minimal inputs and maintenance.
2. Millets are a good source of minerals, dietary fibre, antioxidants and protein.
3. Millets, including sorghum, account for less than 3% of the global grains trade.
Which of the statements given above are correct? [CAPF 2023]
solution:
(D) 1, 2 and 3
Millets (Coarse grains)
also known as superfoods/sri anna as they are good source of minerals, dietary fibre, antioxidants and protein.
Topmost producer - Rajasthan.
Eg. Bajra, Jowar (Sorghum), Ragi (finger millets) (TRICK - BAJRA)
Millets including sorghum account for less than 3% of the global grains trade.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following statements:
Statement 1 : Black soil is considered suitable for growing rain-fed crops.
Statement 2 : Black soil can retain moisture for a prolonged period, aiding crops to survive even in dry seasons.
Which of the following is/are correct? [CAPF 2024]

options.a: Both the statements are correct and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
options.b: Both the statements are correct and statement 2 is NOT the correct explanation of statement 1.
options.c: Statement 1 is correct but statement 2 is false.
options.d: Statement 1 is false but statement 2 is correct.

solution:
(A) Both the statements are correct and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Black soil is considered suitable for growing rain-fed crops as it retains moisture for long period. Black soil becomes sticky when is wet and develops cracks when is dry, so it is called as 'self ploughing soil'.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one among the following statements for black soil is not correct? [CBI 2023]

options.a: It is also known as 'Regur soil'.
options.b: It is generally clayey and impermeable.
options.c: It swells when wet and shrinks when dry.
options.d: It cannot retain water for long time.

solution:
(D) It cannot retain water for long time
See solution of question 23 (level 2).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following is not a basic factor in soil formation? [CBI 2023]
solution:
(C) Human activity
Factors responsible for soil formation - Relief, Parent rock / bedrock, Climate, Vegetation, Time and Relief features.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following is a characteristic feature of Market Gardening? [CISF AC 2021]

options.a: Farms are of very large size.
options.b: Netherlands is an example of Market Gardening.
options.c: It is well-developed in sparsely populated areas of North-West Europe.
options.d: This is practised in the areas where consumers of low income groups are located.

solution:
(B) Netherlands is an example of Market Gardening
Market Gardening - small scale, intensive agricultural practice focused on growing and selling high-value perishable crops, like fruits, vegetables, flowers etc. This is practiced generally in areas where consumers have high purchasing power. Netherlands is an example of market gardening.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The organic component of soil, formed by the decomposition of leaves and other plant materials that adds to the fertility of the soil is called: [CISF AC 2022]
solution:
(A) Humus
Humus is the organic content formed by the decomposition of leaves and other plant materials. It increases the fertility of the soil. It is present in the top soil (A horizon).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one among the following sectors in India consumes the maximum amount of groundwater? [CISF AC 2023]
solution:
(B) Agriculture
Agriculture sector in India consumes the maximum amount of groundwater (approx 89%), followed by domestic use (9%) and industries (2%).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following geographical conditions:
Well-drained, rich, friable loams containing good deal of minerals, temperature between 15°C-28°C and rainfall between 150 cm-250 cm and altitude variations from 600 m-1600 m.
Which one among the following crops is suitable under the above conditions? [CISF AC 2024]
solution:
(B) Coffee
Conditions required for coffee growth : well-drained loamy soil, hot and humid climate, 15°C-28°C temperature, 150-250 cm rainfall.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In which one of the following countries is intensive subsistence agriculture not predominantly practised? [NDA I 2021]
solution:
(C) Canada
Intensive subsistence agriculture is practised on small farms. High doses of biochemical inputs and irrigation are used to obtain higher production. It is practised in countries with high population density and high pressure on land.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following is not a feature of agriculture in India? [NDA I 2021]
solution:
(D) Predominance of cash crops
Features of Indian agriculture :-
Dependent on monsoon.
Labour intensive.
Subsistence farming.
Government support.
Low productivity.
Predominance of food crops.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following pairs of crop and product is/are correctly matched?
1. Food crop : Ragi
2. Cash crop : Jute
3. Plantation crop : Coconut
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [NDA II 2021]
solution:
(C) 1, 2 and 3
Ragi - food crop, Jute - cash crop, Coconut - plantation crop

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following tribes from India's North-East had the earliest known association with cultivation and production of tea? [NDA I 2023]
solution:
(C) Singphos
Singphos tribe of NE India is considered the original tea brewers of India who were associated with tea long before British commercialised it in Assam.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: In the soil-forming regime, which one of the following occurs in a region where evapotranspiration exceeds precipitation significantly? [NDA I 2023]
solution:
(A) Calcification
Calcification - It is a process where calcium carbonate precipitates and accumulated in soil profile. It takes place in arid and semi-arid regions where evapotranspiration exceeds precipitation.
Laterization - It is a soil formation process occurring in hot, humid climate, where chemical weathering leads to accumulation of iron and aluminium oxides and thus forming laterite soil.
Podsolization - It is a process where dissolved organic matter and iron/aluminium ions are moved from upper soil horizon to lower soil horizon.
Gleization - It is a process leading to formation of gley horizon (a layer of grey or bluish soil) due to poor drainage and waterlogged conditions.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
How many of the following pairs of specialized agriculture and its products are correctly matched?
1. Apiculture : Honey
2. Sericulture : Silk
3. Silviculture : Forestry
4. Viticulture : Grapes
Select the correct answer using the code given below: [NDA II 2023]
solution:
(A) 4
Apiculture - bee keeping (honey)
Silviculture - forestry
Sericulture - silk
Viticulture - grapes
Recall other farming also.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following soils is ideal for growing cotton? [NDA II 2023]
solution:
(A) Regur soil
Black soil (Regur soil) is ideal for growing cotton.
Recall all about black soil and cotton.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following statements regarding soils:
1. Soils having pH more than 7 and having high content of sodium and calcium are alkaline soils.
2. Black cotton soil had developed on the Deccan basaltic lava under hot and humid conditions.
3. Laterite soils are characterised by a deep weathered layer from which silica has been leached.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct? [NDA I 2024]
solution:
(D) 1, 2 and 3
Soils having pH more than 7 and having high content of sodium and calcium are called Alkaline soils.
Black soil had developed on the Deccan basaltic lava under hot and humid conditions.
Laterite soils are characterised by a deep weathered layer from which silica has been leached.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following statements regarding Regur soil is/are correct?
1. It is a light coloured, clayey and fertile soil.
2. It is developed on Deccan basaltic lava under hot and humid conditions.
3. Cotton is extensively cultivated in this soil.
Select the correct answer using the code given below. [NDA I 2024]
solution:
(B) 2 and 3
Black soil is dark brown to black in colour. It is clayey and fertile soil, used for cotton production. It is developed on Deccan basaltic lava under hot and humid conditions.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one among the following statements with reference to the black cotton soils of India is NOT correct? [NDA I 2024]

options.a: The black cotton soils are also called 'Regur'.
options.d: These soils have less clay factor and hence can support a wide variety of crops and vegetables.

solution:
(D) These soils have less clay factor and hence can support a wide variety of crops and vegetables.
Black soil is also called 'Regur'. It is highly moisture retentive as it is made of fine particles (clayey). Rich in iron, calcium carbonate, magnesium, potassium, lime, alumina and poor in humus, nitrogen, phosphate.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following is found in appreciable amount in Laterite soil in India? [NDA II 2024]
solution:
(C) Potash
Laterite soils are rich in iron and potash but poor in silica, calcium, phosphate, nitrogen.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Soils which reflect the composition of underlying rocks are always: [GEOSCIENTIST 2022]
solution:
(A) Young
The soils which reflect the composition of underlying rocks are young soil. This is because these soils are formed directly from the weathering and breakdown of parent rock material.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following is correct? [GEOSCIENTIST 2022]

options.a: In humid tropical region, humus content is high in soil.
options.d: In equatorial climates, humus content is high in soil.

solution:
(A) In humid tropical region, humus content is high in soil.
In humid tropical region, humus content is high due to high temperature, high rainfall and abundant vegetation which leads to faster decomposition of organic matter. Humus accumulation is slow in cold climate due to slow rate of decomposition.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The Green Revolution as an agrarian revolution aimed at revolutionizing the production of: [GEOSCIENTIST 2022]
solution:
(A) Food grains
Green revolution was focused on increasing the production of food grains (especially wheat and rice).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following statements about farming in India is/are correct?
1. Dryland farming is confined to the regions having annual rainfall less than 100 cm.
2. Wetland farming regions are often susceptible to flood and soil erosion hazards.
Select the correct answer using the code given below. [GEOSCIENTIST 2023]
solution:
(B) 2 only
Dryland farming is confined to areas having annual rainfall below 75 cm.
Wetland farming regions are often susceptible to flood and soil erosion hazards due to their low lying locations, waterlogged conditions.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The area under millets cultivation is the highest in _______ [GEOSCIENTIST 2023]
solution:
(B) Africa
Africa is the continent with highest area under millet cultivation.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following is the correct sequence of a typical soil profile from top to bottom? [GEOSCIENTIST 2024]

options.a: Topsoil, Humus, Eluviation zone, Subsoil, Weathered parent material
options.b: Subsoil, Topsoil, Humus, Weathered parent material, Eluviation zone
options.c: Humus, Subsoil, Topsoil, Eluviation zone, Weathered parent material
options.d: Humus, Topsoil, Eluviation zone, Subsoil, Weathered parent material

solution:
(D) Humus, Topsoil, Eluviation zone, Subsoil, Weathered parent material
Sequence of soil horizon - Humus (O horizon), Topsoil (A horizon), Eluviated zone (E horizon), Subsoil (B horizon), Parent rock material (C horizon), Bedrock (R horizon).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following statements regarding groundnut cultivation in India is/are correct?
1. Groundnut is a Rabi crop.
2. Gujarat is an important state for Groundnut cultivation.
Select the answer using the code given below: [GEOSCIENTIST 2024]
solution:
(B) 2 only
Groundnut is a kharif crop.
Top producer of groundnut - Gujarat > Rajasthan > Tamil Nadu > Andhra Pradesh.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The black cotton soil of India has been formed due to the weathering of: [IAS 2021]
solution:
(B) Fissure volcanic rock
Black cotton soil has been formed due to the weathering of fissure volcanic rock, particularly basalt, which is common in Deccan plateau.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Among the following, which one is the least water-efficient crop? [IAS 2021]
solution:
(A) Sugarcane
Among the given options, sugarcane is the least water efficient crop, requiring approximately 1500-2000 mm of water per crop cycle.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following States:
1. Andhra Pradesh
2. Kerala
3. Himachal Pradesh
4. Tripura
How many of the above are generally known as tea-producing States? [IAS 2022]
solution:
(C) Only three states
Major tea producing states in India - Assam, West Bengal, Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Tripura, Himachal Pradesh.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Among the following crops, which one is the most important anthropogenic source of both methane and nitrous oxide? [IAS 2022]
solution:
(B) Rice
Rice (paddy) cultivation requires waterlogged conditions which provides conducive environment for production of methane and nitrous oxide.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following statements:
1. India has more arable area than China.
2. The proportion of irrigated area is more in India as compared to China.
3. The average productivity per hectare in Indian agriculture is higher than that in China.
How many of the above statements are correct? [IAS 2023]
solution:
(A) Only one
China has more arable area than India. India has more areas under irrigation than China. The average productivity per hectare is more in China than in India.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following countries are well known as the two largest cocoa producers in the world? [IAS 2024]
solution:
(C) Ivory coast and Ghana
Cote d'Ivoire (Ivory coast) and Ghana are the two largest producers of cocoa in the world, producing over 60% of world's cocoa beans together.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which statements about saline soil in India are correct?
1. They are rich in nitrogen.
2. They occur in arid and semi-arid regions, and in waterlogged and swampy areas.
Select the correct answer: [CDS I 2025]
solution:
(B) 2 only
Saline soils are found in arid and semi-arid regions. They are rich in salts like potassium, sodium, magnesium but poor in nitrogen and organic matter.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which statements about Humus are correct?
1. It is a dark-coloured amorphous substance.
2. Being colloidal, it serves as a nutrient reservoir.
Select the correct answer: [CDS I 2025]
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
Humus is a dark, organic substance formed due to decomposition of plant and animal matter. On being colloidal, it serves as a nutrient reservoir. Montane soil has high humus.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Tertiary coals occur in which of the following states in India? [SSC PHASE XII 21/6/2024]
solution:
(D) Meghalaya
Coal
Also known as Buried sunshine / Black diamond / Black gold.
Reserve : Odisha > Jharkhand > Chhattisgarh. Production : Chhattisgarh > Odisha.
Major coalfields : Jharia, Bokaro, Giridh, Karanpura, Hasdeo, Korba, Neyveli, Ib valley, Jharsuguda, Raniganj.
Tertiary coal is found in Meghalaya, Assam, Arunachal Pradesh, Nagaland.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Koderma Gaya Hazaribagh belt is known for the production of which of the following minerals? [SSC PHASE XII 25/06/2024]
solution:
(D) Mica
Mica
Found in Chota Nagpur plateau, Ajmer, Nellore mica belt. Koderma Gaya Hazaribagh belt of Jharkhand is the leading producer. 4% of Earth's crust is Mica. Mica constitutes of K, Al, Mg, Fe, Si etc.
India is the top most producer of Mica. Within India, Andhra Pradesh, Jharkhand, Bihar are the top producers.
Mica is a non-metallic mineral used in electric and electronic industries due to its excellent di-electric strength, low power loss factor, insulating properties and resistance to high voltage.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following are major coal fields in India? [SSC PHASE XII 26/06/2024]
solution:
(A) Jharia and Raniganj
See solution of question 1 (level 1).

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: NALCO is an Indian government enterprise, dealing with which of the following minerals? [SSC PHASE XII 25/06/2024]
solution:
(B) Bauxite
NALCO (National Aluminium Company) is an Indian government enterprise, dealing with bauxite which is a primary ore of aluminium.
NALCO (National Aluminium Corporation) - 1981, Bhubaneshwar
HINDALCO (Hindustan Aluminium Corporation) - 1958, Mumbai
BALCO (Bharat Aluminium Company) - 1965, New Delhi
MALCO (Madras Aluminium Company) - 1965, Mettur (Tamil Nadu)

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following locations is known for lignite coal production? [SSC JE 05/06/2024]
solution:
(A) Neyveli
Types of Coal
Anthracite – 80-90% carbon, highest quality, hard coal.
Bituminous – 70-80% carbon, coal that has been deep and subjected to increased temperatures.
Lignite – 50-70% carbon, low grade brown coal, soft with high moisture content. Neyveli (Tamil Nadu) is the principal reserve.
Peat – less than 50% carbon, low carbon and high moisture content and low heating capacity.
Anthracite and Bituminous – coking coal (metallurgical coal).
USA – largest coal reserves.
Do you know that coal is the most abundantly found fossil in India?

subject: Indian Geography & Resources

subject: Indian Geography & Resources

solution:

Hirakud captive thermal power plant is located in Odisha.
Shivamogga, which is famous for Iron-ore and Manganese, is located in Karnataka.
Iron Ore
Largest reserves in the world : USA. Largest reserves in India : Odisha. Production : Odisha > Chhattisgarh > Karnataka.
Magnetite - finest iron ore, iron content upto 70%. Most reserves - Karnataka (72%), in world - Australia.
Hematite - industrial iron ore, iron content 50-60%. Most reserves - Odisha.
Iron belts in India - Odisha Jharkhand belt, Durg-Bastar-Chandrapur belt, Maharashtra-Goa belt, Bellary-Chitradurga-Chikmagluru-Tumakuru belt.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The Mumbai High region in the Arabian Sea is known for mining _______. [SSC MTS 04/09/2023]
solution:
(B) Mineral oil

Petroleum
Found in fault traps between porous and non-porous rocks. Associated with anticlines and fault traps in rock formations of tertiary age.
Petroleum producing regions - Mumbai high, Gujarat (Ankleshwar), Assam (oldest oil producing state of India, Digboi, Naharkatiya, Moran-Hugrijan).

solution:

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which of the following statements regarding coal is NOT correct? [SSC CHSL 14/03/2023]

options.d: Metallurgical coal is high grade lignite coal which has a special value for smelting iron in blast furnaces.

solution:
(D) Metallurgical coal is high grade lignite coal which has a special value for smelting iron in blast furnaces.
Metallurgical coal (coking coal) is high grade hard coal, typically bituminous or anthracite.
Anthracite is the highest quality hard coal.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
With reference to India, consider the following statements:

2. Monazite contains thorium.
3. Monazite occurs naturally in the entire Indian coastal sands in India.
4. In India, Government bodies only can process or export monazite.
Which of the statements given above are correct? [IAS 2022]
solution:
(B) 1, 2 and 4 only
Monazite is a source of rare earth elements. It contains thorium. It is found in soils of Kerala. In India, government bodies only can process or export monazite.
Largest reserves of thorium - Andhra Pradesh. Largest producer of thorium - Kerala.
There are 17 rare Earth elements.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following statements:
Statement-I : India, despite having uranium deposits, depends on coal for most of its electricity production.
Statement-II : Uranium, enriched to the extent of at least 60%, is required for the production of electricity.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements? [IAS 2023]

options.a: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is the correct explanation for Statement-I.
options.b: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II is not the correct explanation for Statement-I.
options.c: Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect.
options.d: Statement-I is incorrect but Statement-II is correct.

solution:
(C) Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is incorrect
India does rely heavily on coal for electricity production despite having uranium deposits. Uranium used in commercial nuclear reactors is 3-5% enriched. Highly enriched uranium has a purity of 20% or more and is used in research reactors. Weapon-grade uranium is 90% enriched or more.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Manas, Ranthambore, Kanha, and Sunderbans are: [SSC STENOGRAPHER 12/10/2023]
solution:
(A) National parks
National parks are the areas reserved for wildlife where they can freely use the habitat and natural resources. Total National Parks in India - 106.
Wildlife sanctuaries are the areas where animals are protected from any disturbance to them and their habitat. Total wildlife sanctuaries in India - 573.
Biosphere reserves are large areas of protected land for conservation of wildlife, flora and animal resources and traditional life of the tribals living in the area. Total biosphere reserves in India - 18 (12 under Man And Biosphere programme).
A gene bank is a place where seeds, plants, animals, or DNA are safely stored to protect and preserve genetic material for the future.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: How many biosphere reserves are there in India? [SSC MTS 02/05/2023]
solution:
(B) Eighteen
India has 18 biosphere reserves, out of which 12 are listed under the UNESCO's Man and Biosphere (MAB, 1971) programme.
Cold Desert BR, Saikhova BR, Manas BR, Dihang-Dibang BR, Seshachalam BR, Kachchh BR (largest BR).
Listed in MAB programme - Nanda Devi BR, Khangchendzonga BR (added in 2018, 1st mixed world heritage site), Nokrek BR, Panna BR (latest), Pachmarhi BR, Achanakmar-Amarkantak BR (Chhattisgarh-MP border), Sunderban BR, Simlipal BR, Nilgiri BR (1st under MAB, oldest BR), Agasthyamalai BR, Gulf of Mannar BR, Great Nicobar BR.
Wildlife Protection Act 1972 - 5 schedules, Schedule I : endangered species (Blackbuck, Snow leopard, Asiatic cheetah etc.), Schedule II : Indian cobra, Assam macaque, Himalayan black bear etc.), Schedule III & IV : not endangered, Schedule V : Vermin (disease carrying animals like fruit bat, rats, common crow). Schedule VI : specified plants.
Do you know that Satpura National Park is the first reserve forest of India?

question_text: Which one of the following biosphere reserves comprises islands with estuaries, beaches, coral reefs, salt marshes and mangroves? [CDS II 2021]

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: The 'Ramsar Convention' relates to which one of the following areas? [CDS II 2022]
solution:
(A) Wetland preservation
Ramsar convention (1971, Ramsar Iran) is a convention for the protection of wetlands. It officially entered into force in 1975.
According to it, wetlands are defined as: "areas of marsh, fen, peatland or water, whether natural or artificial, permanent or temporary, with water that is static or flowing, fresh, brackish or salt, including areas of marine water the depth of which at low tide does not exceed six metres".

solution:
(A) Nilgiri
Nilgiri biosphere reserve is the first biosphere reserve of India. It is spread over Kerala, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu and is famous for Nilgiri Tahr.

question_text: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched? [CDS I 2024]

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which organisation publishes worldwide list of endangered species? [CDS I 2024]

options.a: The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN)
options.b: The World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF)

solution:
(A) The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN)
IUCN - International Union for Conservation of Nature
Headquarters - Gland (Switzerland)
Established - 1948
It maintains Red Data Book (records endangered species to raise awareness and protect the diversity).
Categories of Red Data Book - Not Evaluated, Data Deficiency, Least Concern, Near Threatened, Vulnerable, Endangered, Critically Endangered, Extinct in the Wild, Extinct.
CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora) - signed in 1973, entered into force on 1 July 1975, India signed in 1976, aims is to ensure that international trade in specimens of wild animals and plants does not threaten the survival of the species.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Consider the following statements about Ramsar Wetland sites in India:
1. Gahirmatha is the habitat of Olive Ridley Turtles.
2. Chilka lake is the habitat of Irrawaddy Dolphin as its flagship species.
3. Nalsarovar Wetland is also the habitat of Indian Wild Ass (Khur).
4. Tso Moriri Wetland is the habitat and breeding ground of Black-necked Crane.
Which of the statements given above are correct? [CAPF 2023]
solution:
(D) 1, 2, 3 and 4
Gahirmata is the habitat of Olive Ridley Turtles, with other site being Rushikulya.
Chilika lake is the habitat of Irrawaddy Dolphin as its flagship species.
Nalsarovar Wetland is the habitat of many birds and also supports a satellite population of Indian Wild Ass (Khur) which is primarily found in Rann of Kachchh.
Tso Moriri Wetland is the habitat and breeding ground of Black-necked Crane.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
With reference to 'Black Tigers', which of the following statements is/are correct?
1. They are also referred to as Melanistic Tigers
2. In India they are found only in Similipal Tiger Reserve in Odisha
Select the answer using the code given below: [CAPF 2024]
solution:
(C) Both 1 and 2
Black tigers (Melanistic tigers) are not a distinct species but only a rare colour variant of tiger. It is exclusively found in Simlipal Tiger Reserve in Odisha.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text:
Which of the following statements regarding faunal resources of India is/are correct?
1. High degree of endemism has been noticed in the biodiversity hotspots of India.
2. Of the total biosphere reserves in India, the Sundarbans has the largest number of species and Nokrek has the smallest number of species.
Select the answer using the codes given below: [CAPF 2024]
solution:
(A) 1 only
High degree of endemism has been noticed in the biodiversity hotspots of India. It means that the species found here are exclusive to this area. Sunderbans doesn't have the largest number of species and Nokrek doesn't have the smallest number of species.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which one of the following National Parks is an example of protected waterfowl habitat under the Ramsar Convention? [CBI 2023]
solution:
(A) Keoladeo National Park
Keoladeo National Park is a protected waterfowl habitat under Ramsar convention.
Waterfowl habitat refers to the wetland and aquatic environments where birds like ducks, geese, and swans, among others, live, breed, and feed. These habitats include marshes, rivers, ponds, lakes, and coastal areas.

subject: Indian Geography & Resources
question_text: Which Biosphere Reserve is spread over three states in India? [CDS I 2025]
solution:
(B) Nilgiris
Nilgiri biosphere reserve is spread across Kerala, Tamilnadu and Karnataka. It is the oldest biosphere reserve and the first biosphere reserve under MAB programme of UNESCO.
Total Biosphere Reserves - 18 (12 under MAB programme).
